title: Female Urinary Catheterisation
oneLiner: Aseptic urethral catheter insertion to drain the bladder; indicated for acute/chronic retention, accurate output monitoring, or incontinence management.

sections.Killers First — What Will Kill Them In T:
- **Urethral rupture / false passage** — severe pain on insertion, sudden resistance then no urine, haematuria at meatus. Do NOT inflate balloon; stop immediately.
- **Bacteraemia/sepsis from manipulation** — flushed face, rigors, hypotension shortly after catheterisation (especially if pre-existing UTI). Ensure adequate hydration and have IV antibiotics ready.
- **Post-obstructive diuresis** — massive polyuria after relief of chronic retention → hypovolaemia, hypotension, electrolyte disturbances. Monitor hourly urine output and replace losses.
sections.Presentation:
- **Indications (why you are called):** acute retention (inability to void, suprapubic pain), chronic retention (distended bladder, overflow incontinence, often painless), accurate urine output monitoring (sepsis, AKI, ICU), incontinence management, bladder irrigation, pre-operative.
- **Key signs:** palpable/full bladder on exam, suprapubic dullness, patient in distress, or new anuria/oliguria.
- **Procedure-specific findings:** normal urethral meatus, absence of vaginal atrophy or stricture, prior catheter history.
sections.Differential Diagnosis:
- **Acute retention vs anuria** — bladder is empty in anuria (no urine production), full in retention. Key: bladder scan shows <50ml in anuria, >300ml in retention.
- **Acute retention vs overflow incontinence** — overflow: constant dribbling with a large, non-tender bladder (chronic). Acute: sudden, painful inability to void.
- **Catheterisation failure:**
  - Urethral stricture (rare in females) → guidewire or suprapubic.
  - Vaginal insertion → check balloon not inflated before confirming position.
  - Bladder neck obstruction → gentle rotation of catheter.
- **Splitter:** Bedside bladder ultrasound — full bladder = true retention; empty = anuria or upper tract obstruction.
sections.Investigations:
- **Bedside:**
  - Bladder scan (post-void residual or pre-catheter volume) to confirm retention.
  - Urine dipstick (if possible) for evidence of UTI before instrumentation.
  - Ensure catheter position BEFORE inflating balloon — aspirate urine (often just a few ml) or use ultrasound.
- **Bloods:**
  - U&Es, creatinine (assess AKI, post-obstructive diuresis risk).
  - FBC (infection risk).
- **Imaging:**
  - Renal ultrasound if suspect upper tract obstruction (e.g. known stone, hydronephrosis).
- **Special tests:** none routine for simple catheterisation.
- **Never-miss test:** **Bladder ultrasound** to distinguish retention from anuria — omitting this may lead to unnecessary catheterisation in anuric patient, risking trauma.
sections.Management:
- **Immediate (first hour / ED):**
  - Confirm indication. Ensure adequate consent (verbal usually sufficient for emergency).
  - Prepare sterile field: clean with chlorhexidine/water, lubricate generously with lidocaine gel.
  - Use smallest appropriate catheter (12–14 Fr Foley) with 10 ml balloon.
  - Insert until urine flows, then advance 2–3 cm further to avoid balloon in urethra.
  - Inflate balloon slowly — if patient reports pain, stop and deflate (suspect urethral placement).
  - Secure catheter to thigh with drainage bag system.
  - Record: catheter size, balloon volume, residue volume, urine appearance, difficulty.
- **Ward (first 24–72h):**
  - Monitor urine output hourly if indicated (sepsis, AKI, fluid balance).
  - Document fluid balance chart.
  - Remove catheter as soon as no longer needed (reduce CAUTI risk).
  - Consider catheter care bundle: daily meatal hygiene, ensure bag below bladder, closed drainage.
- **Long-term / outpatient:**
  - If long-term catheter: plan for changes every 4–6 weeks, consider suprapubic for recurrent urethral trauma.
  - Refer to continence team if indicated.
- **Default Orders (if admitted):**
  - Fluids: encourage oral hydration (1.5–2 L/day) unless fluid restricted.
  - VTE prophylaxis: standard (catheter itself increases infection risk, not VTE).
  - Diet: NBM only if procedure under sedation.
  - Observations: hourly urine output for first 4h, then 4-hourly if stable.
sections.Drugs & Doses:
- **Lubricant gel:** Instillagel (lidocaine 2% + chlorhexidine) 6–11 ml per urethra — insert slowly.
- **Prophylactic antibiotics (if indicated):** e.g. prosthetic heart valve, renal transplant, recent UTI — give single dose per local policy. [Check local policy] for agent (e.g. gentamicin 3 mg/kg IV once).
- **Common alternative:** no routine antibiotic for simple catheterisation.
- **Key contraindications:** lidocaine allergy (use plain lubricant).
- **Renal/hepatic adjustment:** none for topical lidocaine.
- **Most dangerous dose error:** **Balloon inflation in urethra** — if you inflate without urine return, you risk urethral rupture. Always aspirate urine first (or use ultrasound).
sections.Complications:
- **Acute:**
  - Urethral trauma / false passage — stop, call urology.
  - Haematuria — usually self-limiting; if heavy with clots → bladder irrigation.
  - Catheter-associated UTI (CAUTI) — most common, especially after >48h.
  - Post-obstructive diuresis — watch for polyuria >200 ml/hour, monitor U&Es.
  - Autonomic dysreflexia (in spinal cord injury patients) — headache, bradycardia, hypertension; treat by stopping procedure, check bladder distension.
- **Chronic:**
  - Recurrent CAUTI / pyelonephritis.
  - Bladder stones, encrustation, catheter blockage.
  - Urethral erosion (long-term catheters).
  - Loss of bladder tone (if long-term defunctioned).
sections.Prognosis:
- Short-term catheterisation (<48h): minimal risk, removal leads to normal voiding in most.
- Long-term catheter: up to 50% will develop CAUTI; higher morbidity in elderly/frail.
sections.Pearls (positive actions):
- **Always use generous lubrication** — reduces trauma and pain. Wait 2–3 minutes for gel to take effect.
- **Use smallest catheter (12–14 Fr)** — prevents urethral stretching and later incontinence.
- **Confirm position by ultrasound** if urine aspiration fails (especially in dehydrated patients) — avoids balloon inflation in urethra.
sections.Pitfalls (Errors To Avoid):
- **Inflating balloon without confirming bladder position** — most dangerous mistake.
- **Using too large a catheter** (e.g. 18 Fr routinely) — unnecessary trauma and eventual incontinence.
- **Leaving catheter in longer than necessary** — major contributor to healthcare-associated UTI.
- **Forgetting to secure catheter** — traction causes balloon to rest at bladder neck, leading to bypassing and pain.
sections.Safety Netting (If Patient Goes Home):
- **Return if:** Fever/chills/rigors, suprapubic pain, foul-smelling urine, visible blood in urine, catheter stops draining or bypasses, catheter falls out, inability to pass urine after removal.
- **Follow-up:** If long-term catheter, district nurse for regular changes; if acute retention, urology outpatient review within 2 weeks.
sections.Escalation Criteria:
- **Call senior / on-call urology if:**
  - Failed catheterisation after 2 attempts (consider suprapubic catheter).
  - Significant haematuria with clots → consider irrigation.
  - Suspected false passage → stop, get urology advice.
- **Call ICU/crash team if:**
  - NEWS2 ≥5 (especially hypotension, high lactate) post-procedure → suspect urosepsis.
  - Anuria after catheter insertion (catheter may be in incorrect position or patient has anuria; urgent bladder scan and U&Es).

rawMarkdown:
## Female Urinary Catheterisation

**One-liner:** Aseptic urethral catheter insertion to drain the bladder; indicated for acute/chronic retention, accurate output monitoring, or incontinence management.

### Killers First — What Will Kill Them In The First Hour?
- **Urethral rupture / false passage** — severe pain on insertion, sudden resistance then no urine, haematuria at meatus. Do NOT inflate balloon; stop immediately.
- **Bacteraemia/sepsis from manipulation** — flushed face, rigors, hypotension shortly after catheterisation (especially if pre-existing UTI). Ensure adequate hydration and have IV antibiotics ready.
- **Post-obstructive diuresis** — massive polyuria after relief of chronic retention → hypovolaemia, hypotension, electrolyte disturbances. Monitor hourly urine output and replace losses.

### Presentation
- **Indications (why you are called):** acute retention (inability to void, suprapubic pain), chronic retention (distended bladder, overflow incontinence, often painless), accurate urine output monitoring (sepsis, AKI, ICU), incontinence management, bladder irrigation, pre-operative.
- **Key signs:** palpable/full bladder on exam, suprapubic dullness, patient in distress, or new anuria/oliguria.
- **Procedure-specific findings:** normal urethral meatus, absence of vaginal atrophy or stricture, prior catheter history.

### Differential Diagnosis
- **Acute retention vs anuria** — bladder is empty in anuria (no urine production), full in retention. Key: bladder scan shows <50ml in anuria, >300ml in retention.
- **Acute retention vs overflow incontinence** — overflow: constant dribbling with a large, non-tender bladder (chronic). Acute: sudden, painful inability to void.
- **Catheterisation failure:**
  - Urethral stricture (rare in females) → guidewire or suprapubic.
  - Vaginal insertion → check balloon not inflated before confirming position.
  - Bladder neck obstruction → gentle rotation of catheter.
- **Splitter:** Bedside bladder ultrasound — full bladder = true retention; empty = anuria or upper tract obstruction.

### Investigations
- **Bedside:**
  - Bladder scan (post-void residual or pre-catheter volume) to confirm retention.
  - Urine dipstick (if possible) for evidence of UTI before instrumentation.
  - Ensure catheter position BEFORE inflating balloon — aspirate urine (often just a few ml) or use ultrasound.
- **Bloods:**
  - U&Es, creatinine (assess AKI, post-obstructive diuresis risk).
  - FBC (infection risk).
- **Imaging:**
  - Renal ultrasound if suspect upper tract obstruction (e.g. known stone, hydronephrosis).
- **Special tests:** none routine for simple catheterisation.
- **Never-miss test:** **Bladder ultrasound** to distinguish retention from anuria — omitting this may lead to unnecessary catheterisation in anuric patient, risking trauma.

### Management
- **Immediate (first hour / ED):**
  - Confirm indication. Ensure adequate consent (verbal usually sufficient for emergency).
  - Prepare sterile field: clean with chlorhexidine/water, lubricate generously with lidocaine gel.
  - Use smallest appropriate catheter (12–14 Fr Foley) with 10 ml balloon.
  - Insert until urine flows, then advance 2–3 cm further to avoid balloon in urethra.
  - Inflate balloon slowly — if patient reports pain, stop and deflate (suspect urethral placement).
  - Secure catheter to thigh with drainage bag system.
  - Record: catheter size, balloon volume, residue volume, urine appearance, difficulty.
- **Ward (first 24–72h):**
  - Monitor urine output hourly if indicated (sepsis, AKI, fluid balance).
  - Document fluid balance chart.
  - Remove catheter as soon as no longer needed (reduce CAUTI risk).
  - Consider catheter care bundle: daily meatal hygiene, ensure bag below bladder, closed drainage.
- **Long-term / outpatient:**
  - If long-term catheter: plan for changes every 4–6 weeks, consider suprapubic for recurrent urethral trauma.
  - Refer to continence team if indicated.
- **Default Orders (if admitted):**
  - Fluids: encourage oral hydration (1.5–2 L/day) unless fluid restricted.
  - VTE prophylaxis: standard (catheter itself increases infection risk, not VTE).
  - Diet: NBM only if procedure under sedation.
  - Observations: hourly urine output for first 4h, then 4-hourly if stable.

### Drugs & Doses
- **Lubricant gel:** Instillagel (lidocaine 2% + chlorhexidine) 6–11 ml per urethra — insert slowly.
- **Prophylactic antibiotics (if indicated):** e.g. prosthetic heart valve, renal transplant, recent UTI — give single dose per local policy. [Check local policy] for agent (e.g. gentamicin 3 mg/kg IV once).
- **Common alternative:** no routine antibiotic for simple catheterisation.
- **Key contraindications:** lidocaine allergy (use plain lubricant).
- **Renal/hepatic adjustment:** none for topical lidocaine.
- **Most dangerous dose error:** **Balloon inflation in urethra** — if you inflate without urine return, you risk urethral rupture. Always aspirate urine first (or use ultrasound).

### Complications
- **Acute:**
  - Urethral trauma / false passage — stop, call urology.
  - Haematuria — usually self-limiting; if heavy with clots → bladder irrigation.
  - Catheter-associated UTI (CAUTI) — most common, especially after >48h.
  - Post-obstructive diuresis — watch for polyuria >200 ml/hour, monitor U&Es.
  - Autonomic dysreflexia (in spinal cord injury patients) — headache, bradycardia, hypertension; treat by stopping procedure, check bladder distension.
- **Chronic:**
  - Recurrent CAUTI / pyelonephritis.
  - Bladder stones, encrustation, catheter blockage.
  - Urethral erosion (long-term catheters).
  - Loss of bladder tone (if long-term defunctioned).

### Prognosis
- Short-term catheterisation (<48h): minimal risk, removal leads to normal voiding in most.
- Long-term catheter: up to 50% will develop CAUTI; higher morbidity in elderly/frail.

### Pearls (positive actions)
- **Always use generous lubrication** — reduces trauma and pain. Wait 2–3 minutes for gel to take effect.
- **Use smallest catheter (12–14 Fr)** — prevents urethral stretching and later incontinence.
- **Confirm position by ultrasound** if urine aspiration fails (especially in dehydrated patients) — avoids balloon inflation in urethra.

### Pitfalls (Errors To Avoid)
- **Inflating balloon without confirming bladder position** — most dangerous mistake.
- **Using too large a catheter** (e.g. 18 Fr routinely) — unnecessary trauma and eventual incontinence.
- **Leaving catheter in longer than necessary** — major contributor to healthcare-associated UTI.
- **Forgetting to secure catheter** — traction causes balloon to rest at bladder neck, leading to bypassing and pain.

### Safety Netting (If Patient Goes Home)
- **Return if:** Fever/chills/rigors, suprapubic pain, foul-smelling urine, visible blood in urine, catheter stops draining or bypasses, catheter falls out, inability to pass urine after removal.
- **Follow-up:** If long-term catheter, district nurse for regular changes; if acute retention, urology outpatient review within 2 weeks.

### Escalation Criteria
- **Call senior / on-call urology if:**
  - Failed catheterisation after 2 attempts (consider suprapubic catheter).
  - Significant haematuria with clots → consider irrigation.
  - Suspected false passage → stop, get urology advice.
- **Call ICU/crash team if:**
  - NEWS2 ≥5 (especially hypotension, high lactate) post-procedure → suspect urosepsis.
  - Anuria after catheter insertion (catheter may be in incorrect position or patient has anuria; urgent bladder scan and U&Es).
